title: Wealth Transfer Techniques
description: Understand GRATs and their tax advantages, explain installment sales to IDGTs, apply QPRTs for residence transfers, and compare freeze techniques.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Advanced wealth transfer techniques allow clients to transfer appreciating assets to the next generation at reduced gift tax cost. These strategies leverage the difference between actual growth and IRS-assumed growth rates.

sections.title: Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT)
sections.content:
How GRATs Work:
1. Grantor transfers assets to irrevocable trust
2. Grantor receives fixed annuity payments for term
3. At term end, remainder passes to beneficiaries (or trust)
4. Gift value = Transfer value - Present value of retained annuity

sections.title: GRAT Calculation
sections.content:
Taxable Gift = FMV of Assets - PV of Annuity Stream

Zeroed-Out GRAT: Annuity set so taxable gift = approximately $0. Uses IRC §7520 rate (120% of mid-term AFR). If assets grow faster than 7520 rate, excess passes gift-tax-free.

sections.title: GRAT Success
sections.content: If assets grow at 10% (vs. 5% assumed rate): After 5 years of annuities and growth, ~$2M+ passes to beneficiaries tax-free!

sections.title: GRAT Risks
sections.content: Grantor must survive the term (mortality risk). No estate tax benefit if dies during term—assets return to estate. Rolling GRATs (multiple short-term 2-year GRATs) mitigate mortality risk by capturing appreciation in each period.

sections.title: Qualified Personal Residence Trust (QPRT)
sections.content:
Purpose: Transfer residence at reduced gift tax value while continuing to live there.

How It Works:
1. Transfer home to irrevocable trust
2. Reserve right to live in home for term of years
3. At term end, home passes to beneficiaries
4. Gift value = Home value - PV of retained use

Benefits: Significant gift tax discount (20-70% depending on term/age); removes appreciation from estate; continue living in home during trust term.

Post-Term Options: Move out (property belongs to beneficiaries); rent at fair market rate (additional estate reduction); beneficiaries can allow continued residence.

sections.title: QPRT Mortality Risk
sections.content: If grantor dies during term: Full FMV included in gross estate; no benefit achieved (but no worse off than not doing the QPRT).

sections.title: Installment Sales to IDGTs
sections.content:
Concept: Sell appreciating assets to grantor trust in exchange for installment note.

Why "Intentionally Defective"? Trust is "defective" for income tax (grantor pays tax) but complete for gift/estate tax (assets removed from estate).

Benefits:
1. No gain recognized on sale (sale to self for income tax)
2. Interest payments are not income to grantor
3. Appreciation shifts to beneficiaries
4. Grantor paying income tax = additional gift without gift tax

sections.title: IDGT Structure
sections.content:
Structure:
1. Create IDGT, seed with gift (10% of sale value)
2. Sell asset to trust for installment note (AFR interest)
3. Trust pays principal + interest over term
4. Asset appreciates inside trust; appreciation avoids transfer tax

sections.title: IDGT Result
sections.content: If asset grows 8%, trust keeps excess appreciation (~$4M over term) tax-free!

sections.title: Family Limited Partnerships (FLPs)
sections.content:
Structure: Parents contribute assets to partnership; parents retain general partner interest (1-2%); children receive limited partner interests (gift/sale).

Benefits:
1. Valuation discounts (minority, marketability)
2. Control retained through GP interest
3. Creditor protection for limited partners
4. Wealth shift through discounted gifts

Legitimate Purposes: Consolidated management; asset protection; family governance; intergenerational business succession.

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content: IRS Scrutiny of FLPs: Must have legitimate non-tax business purpose; must observe formalities; cannot retain too much control; death-bed transfers are problematic.

sections.title: Mnemonic: GIFT
sections.content: Wealth Transfer Techniques: GRATs, IDGTs, FLPs, Trusts (QPRTs)

sections.title: Summary
items: GRATs transfer appreciation above 7520 rate tax-free
Zeroed-out GRATs have minimal gift tax cost
QPRTs transfer residence at substantial discount
IDGT sales freeze value while shifting appreciation
FLPs combine control retention with valuation discounts

knowledgeCheck.question: Mrs. Gable transfers $1,000,000 of publicly traded stock to a 5-year GRAT, retaining an annuity payment of $210,000 per year. The applicable IRC §7520 rate is 4%. At the end of the 5-year term, the assets remaining in the GRAT will pass to her children. Assuming Mrs. Gable survives the GRAT term, what is the amount of the taxable gift?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The taxable gift is calculated as the fair market value of the assets transferred to the GRAT less the present value of the retained annuity.  In this case, the PV of the annuity is approximately $844,500.  $1,000,000-$844,500 = $155,500. The question contains an irrelevant detail. It asks how much is the taxable gift, and then states 'At the end of the 5-year term, the assets remaining in the GRAT will pass to her children' Since the amount of assets remaining in the GRAT at the end of the term is irrelevant to calculation of the taxable gift, the amount of assets remaining in the GRAT at the end of the term is not a factor in calculating the taxable gift.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Recall the formula: Taxable Gift = FMV of Assets - PV of Annuity Stream. Use a financial calculator or PV annuity table to calculate the present value of the annuity stream.

reveal.question: List the four key steps in the GRAT process.
reveal.answer:
1. Grantor transfers assets to an irrevocable trust.
2. Grantor receives fixed annuity payments for a defined term.
3. At the end of the term, the remaining assets pass to the beneficiaries (or another trust).
4. The taxable gift is calculated as the transfer value of the assets minus the present value of the retained annuity.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements regarding a zeroed-out GRAT is MOST accurate?
options: A zeroed-out GRAT eliminates all gift tax implications, regardless of asset performance.
A zeroed-out GRAT is always the most tax-efficient wealth transfer strategy.
A zeroed-out GRAT is structured so that the present value of the retained annuity is approximately equal to the value of the assets transferred, resulting in a minimal taxable gift.
A zeroed-out GRAT guarantees that the beneficiaries will receive the assets gift-tax-free.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A zeroed-out GRAT aims for a minimal taxable gift by setting the annuity payments such that their present value nearly equals the transferred asset value. It doesn't eliminate all gift tax (especially if the grantor dies during the term), isn't always the best strategy, and doesn't guarantee gift-tax-free transfer (asset growth must exceed the §7520 rate).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what 'zeroed-out' implies in terms of the taxable gift.

rows.label: Asset Growth
values: Growth exceeding the §7520 rate passes gift tax-free.
All future appreciation belongs to the recipient.

title: Business Succession Planning
description: Identify business succession options, understand buy-sell agreements, and apply IRC §303 and §6166 provisions.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Business succession planning ensures smooth ownership transitions at death, disability, or retirement. Proper planning protects business value and family wealth.

sections.title: Internal Succession Options
sections.content:
Family Transfer: Gifts (annual exclusion, exemption); sales (installment, IDGT); bequests (estate plan).

Management/Employee Buyout: Key employees purchase business; often structured with seller financing; ESOP (Employee Stock Ownership Plan).

sections.title: External Succession Options
sections.content:
Third-Party Sale: Strategic buyer (competitor, industry player); financial buyer (private equity); highest value but loss of legacy.

Merger: Combine with complementary business; may retain some ownership/involvement.

Liquidation: Wind down operations; sell assets; last resort option.

sections.title: Buy-Sell Agreements
sections.content:
Purpose: Predetermined terms for ownership transfers upon triggering events.

Triggering Events: Death; disability; retirement; divorce; termination of employment; bankruptcy.

sections.title: Types of Buy-Sell Agreements
rows: Remaining owners buy interest from departing owner
Business buys back departing owner's interest
Simpler with many owners; no basis step-up

sections.title: Funding Buy-Sell Agreements
sections.content:
Life Insurance: Most common funding mechanism; provides immediate liquidity at death; premiums are not deductible.

Disability Insurance: Funds buyout at disability; usually with installment payments.

Sinking Fund: Accumulate cash over time; may be insufficient at early death.

sections.title: Cross-Purchase Insurance
sections.content: For n owners, you need n × (n-1) policies. Example: 3 equal shareholders need 6 policies—each owner buys a policy on each other owner.

sections.title: IRC §303 Redemption
sections.content:
Purpose: Allow estate to redeem stock to pay death taxes and expenses without dividend treatment.

Requirements:
1. Stock value > 35% of adjusted gross estate
2. Redemption limited to death taxes + funeral/admin expenses
3. Must occur within Section 303 time period

Benefits: Capital gain treatment (not dividend); if basis stepped up at death, little or no gain; provides liquidity without income tax.

sections.title: IRC §6166 Installment Payment
sections.content:
Purpose: Allow estate to pay estate tax attributable to closely-held business over time.

Requirements:
1. Closely-held business interest > 35% of adjusted gross estate
2. Decedent must be active in business
3. Business must continue operating

Payment Terms: 4-year deferral (interest only); 10-year installment payments; special 2% interest rate on first $750K deferred tax; regular interest on balance.

Benefits: Preserves business liquidity; avoids forced sale of business; low interest rate on portion.

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content: §6166 Acceleration Events (full payment due): 50% or more of business sold; business income substantially reduced; missed payments.

sections.title: Family Business Transition Strategies
items: Lifetime Transfers: Annual exclusion gifts, GRATs, IDGT sales, FLP/LLC for discounts
Control Transition: Voting/non-voting structure—transfer non-voting shares (discounted), retain voting shares (control)
Management Succession: Identify and develop successors; create employment agreements; establish governance structure
Equalizing Estates: Life insurance for inactive children; non-business assets to inactive children; installment sale to active child

sections.title: Mnemonic: CES
sections.content: Buy-Sell Types: Cross-purchase, Entity purchase, (Wait and) See

sections.title: Summary
items: Buy-sell agreements establish terms for ownership transitions
Cross-purchase provides basis step-up; entity purchase is simpler
IRC §303 allows stock redemption to pay estate taxes at capital gain rates
IRC §6166 permits 14-year installment payment of estate taxes
Voting/non-voting structures enable control retention during lifetime transfers

knowledgeCheck.question: A business owner wants to transfer their business to their children while minimizing gift tax implications and retaining some control. Which of the following internal succession options would be MOST suitable?
options: A. Outright gift of the business using the annual exclusion only.
B. Sale of the business to the children using an Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT).
C. Bequest of the business through the owner's will.
D. Management Buyout
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An IDGT allows the owner to sell the business to a trust for the benefit of their children. The sale is not subject to gift tax, and the owner can structure the trust to retain some control. Using just the annual exclusion would take too long and not transfer the full business value efficiently. A bequest would trigger estate taxes, and the owner loses control immediately. A Management Buyout is not an internal family succession option.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the tax implications and control retained by the business owner.

reveal.question: Briefly describe the purpose of IRC §303.
reveal.answer: IRC §303 allows an estate to redeem stock from a closely held business to pay estate taxes and administration expenses without the redemption being treated as a dividend. The value of the stock must exceed 35% of the adjusted gross estate.

sections.title: Buy-Sell Agreements: Entity Purchase vs. Cross Purchase

rows.label: Parties Involved
values: Business entity and owners
Owners and owners

rows.label: Purchase of Shares
values: Business purchases shares from the deceased/departing owner.
Remaining owners purchase shares from the deceased/departing owner.

rows.label: Tax Implications
values: Can be simpler to administer but may have corporate alternative minimum tax (AMT) issues and no step-up in basis for surviving owners.
More complex to administer but surviving owners get a step-up in basis.

rows.label: Funding
values: Business uses its assets or life insurance to fund the purchase.
Owners use their personal assets or life insurance to fund the purchase.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following external succession options would MOST likely result in the highest sale price for the business, but also the greatest loss of the business's legacy?
options: A. Merger with a complementary business.
B. Sale to a strategic buyer (e.g., a competitor).
C. Sale to a financial buyer (e.g., private equity firm).
D. Liquidation of the business's assets.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A strategic buyer is often willing to pay a premium to acquire a competitor or a business that complements its existing operations. However, strategic buyers are often focused on maximizing profits and may be less concerned with preserving the business's legacy. Mergers can preserve some legacy, sale to a financial buyer may or may not, and liquidation almost certainly destroys legacy.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the motivations of each type of buyer.

title: Charitable Estate Planning
description: Integrate charitable giving with estate planning, apply charitable remainder and lead trusts, and understand private foundations vs. donor-advised funds.
topics: Income, gift, and estate tax benefits

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Charitable estate planning combines philanthropic goals with tax benefits. Understanding charitable giving vehicles helps advisors maximize both family wealth transfer and charitable impact.

sections.title: Estate Tax Charitable Deduction
sections.content: Unlimited deduction for bequests to charity. Reduces taxable estate dollar-for-dollar. Charity receives full amount (no estate tax).

sections.title: IRD Strategy
sections.content:
Best assets to leave to charity: IRAs, 401(k)s (would be taxed as income to heirs); appreciated stock (charity avoids capital gains).

Strategy: Leave IRD to charity; leave stepped-up assets to family.

sections.title: Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT)
sections.content:
Structure:
1. Donor transfers assets to irrevocable trust
2. Donor (or others) receive income for life or term
3. Charity receives remainder at trust termination

Types:
• CRAT (Charitable Remainder Annuity Trust): Fixed dollar payment each year; no additional contributions; payment: 5-50% of initial value.
• CRUT (Charitable Remainder Unitrust): Percentage of annual trust value; may allow additional contributions; payment: 5-50% of annual value.

sections.title: CRT Tax Benefits
rows: Trust sells asset, no gain to donor

sections.title: CRT Requirements
sections.content:
Deduction = FMV of Gift - PV of Retained Income Interest

Requirements: Remainder must be at least 10% of initial value; term: life or up to 20 years; payout: 5-50% annually.

sections.title: Charitable Lead Trust (CLT)
sections.content:
Structure: Income to charity for term; remainder to family.

Purpose: Transfer assets to family at reduced gift/estate tax cost.

Types:
• Grantor CLT: Immediate income tax deduction; grantor taxed on trust income annually.
• Non-Grantor CLT: No income tax deduction; trust pays tax on income not distributed to charity.

Gift/Estate Tax Calculation: Taxable Gift = FMV of Assets - PV of Charity's Income Interest

sections.title: Memory Aid
sections.content:
CRT = Charity gets Remainder (you get income first)
CLT = Charity Leads (charity gets income first)

sections.title: Private Foundations
sections.content:
Characteristics: Separate legal entity (nonprofit); family can control and manage; make grants to charities; complex rules and excise taxes.

Benefits: Family involvement in philanthropy; perpetual existence; employment for family members; control over grant-making.

Drawbacks: Administrative burden; 5% annual distribution requirement; excise taxes (1.39% on investment income); lower deduction limits (30% AGI vs. 60%).

sections.title: Donor-Advised Funds (DAF)
sections.content:
Characteristics: Account at public charity; immediate deduction at contribution; advise on grants (no control); no minimum distribution requirement.

Benefits: Simple administration (no legal entity); full public charity deduction limits; lower costs than foundation; immediate deduction, distribute later.

sections.title: Wealth Replacement Strategy
sections.content:
Challenge: Assets left to charity are not available for family.

Solution: Wealth Replacement Trust
1. Create ILIT (Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust)
2. Use tax savings from charitable gift to pay premiums
3. Insurance replaces "lost" inheritance
4. Insurance proceeds estate-tax-free

Example: Donate $1M to charity via CRT; tax savings: ~$300K; use savings to fund ILIT purchasing $1M policy; family receives same value, tax-free.

sections.title: Summary
items: Leave IRD assets (IRAs) to charity; stepped-up assets to family
CRTs provide income to donor, remainder to charity
CLTs provide income to charity, remainder to family at reduced tax
DAFs offer simplicity; private foundations offer control
Wealth replacement trusts (ILITs) can replace charitable gifts to family

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding a Charitable Remainder Annuity Trust (CRAT)?
options: The annual payment to the non-charitable beneficiary is a fixed percentage of the trust assets, revalued annually.
Additional contributions to the trust are permitted after the initial funding.
The annual payment to the non-charitable beneficiary is a fixed dollar amount, and no additional contributions are allowed.
The charity receives income during the term of the trust.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A CRAT provides a fixed dollar payment to the non-charitable beneficiary each year. No additional contributions are permitted after the initial funding. A Charitable Remainder Unitrust (CRUT) pays a fixed percentage of the trust assets, revalued annually, and may allow additional contributions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the difference between 'annuity' and 'unitrust' in the context of CRTs.

reveal.question: What type of assets are generally most suitable to leave to a charity in an estate plan, and why?
reveal.answer: Income in Respect of a Decedent (IRD) assets, such as IRAs and 401(k)s, are generally most suitable to leave to charity. This is because these assets would be taxed as income to heirs, but a charity can receive them tax-free. Also, appreciated stock can be given to charity to avoid the capital gains tax consequences.

rows.label: Control
values: Donor has significant control over investment and grantmaking decisions.
Sponsoring organization has ultimate control; donor provides recommendations.

rows.label: Administrative Burden
values: Significant administrative burden, including compliance and reporting requirements.
Minimal administrative burden; sponsoring organization handles all administrative tasks.

rows.label: Tax Deduction
values: Generally, deduction limited to 30% of adjusted gross income (AGI) for cash and 20% for appreciated property.
Generally, deduction limited to 60% of adjusted gross income (AGI) for cash and 30% for appreciated property.

rows.label: Cost
values: Higher costs to establish and maintain.
Lower costs to establish and maintain.

title: Estate Planning for Special Situations
description: Plan for blended families, address non-citizen spouse issues, structure planning for minor children, and navigate domestic partner considerations.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Special situations require tailored estate planning approaches. This lesson covers common scenarios requiring specialized strategies: blended families, non-citizen spouses, minor children, and unmarried partners.

sections.title: Blended Family Challenges
sections.content:
Common Challenges:
• Competing interests (spouse vs. children from prior marriage)
• Desire to provide for current spouse AND ensure children inherit
• Step-children (no legal inheritance rights)
• Potential for family conflict

sections.title: QTIP Trust for Blended Families
sections.content:
Preferred tool for blended families:
1. Assets pass to QTIP trust at first death
2. Surviving spouse receives all income for life
3. At spouse's death, remainder passes to children from first marriage
4. Qualifies for marital deduction

sections.title: Other Blended Family Strategies
items: Prenuptial agreements: Define separate vs. marital property; waive elective share rights; establish inheritance expectations
Life insurance: Name children as beneficiaries; separate from estate assets for spouse
Separate trusts: Trust for spouse (marital trust); trust for children (family trust)

sections.title: Non-Citizen Spouse Problem
sections.content: Marital deduction NOT available for non-citizen spouse. Concern: Spouse leaves US, takes assets beyond US tax jurisdiction.

sections.title: Qualified Domestic Trust (QDOT)
sections.content:
Requirements:
1. At least one US trustee (or US bank)
2. Trustee must withhold estate tax on principal distributions
3. Must meet regulatory requirements
4. Election made on estate tax return

How It Works: Assets pass to QDOT instead of outright to spouse; marital deduction allowed for assets in QDOT; estate tax deferred until distributions from QDOT or spouse's death.

sections.title: Non-Citizen Spouse Gift Exclusion
sections.content: $190,000 annual exclusion for gifts to non-citizen spouse (2026)—much higher than regular $19,000 exclusion.

sections.title: Planning for Minor Children
sections.content:
Guardianship Designation:
• Guardianship of Person: Who raises child; court approves, but will respects wishes; name alternates.
• Guardianship of Property: Who manages assets for child; often separate from personal guardian.

sections.title: Trusts for Minors
rows: Simple custodial account; custodian manages until age 18-21; assets belong to child; no asset protection
Qualifies for annual exclusion; income may be accumulated; must distribute at age 21
Qualifies for annual exclusion; more flexible terms; can last beyond age 21

sections.title: Age-Based Distribution Provisions
sections.content:
Common trust provisions:
• 1/3 at age 25
• 1/2 of remainder at age 30
• Balance at age 35

Or: Income at 25, principal at 35.

Incentive Trusts: Distributions tied to behavior (matching employment income; education completion bonuses; avoiding substance abuse). Caution: Subjective criteria can create conflict.

sections.title: Unmarried/Domestic Partner Challenges
sections.content:
Challenges:
• No marital deduction
• No automatic inheritance rights
• Potential family conflict
• Healthcare decision concerns

sections.title: Unmarried Partner Strategies
items: Wills and trusts essential: Cannot rely on intestacy; must explicitly name partner; consider family challenge risk
Beneficiary designations: Update retirement accounts; update life insurance; most reliable transfer method
Healthcare documents: Healthcare proxy critical; hospital visitation authorization; HIPAA authorization
Property ownership: Joint tenancy with right of survivorship; community property (if applicable)

sections.title: Same-Sex Marriage
sections.content: Post-Obergefell: Legal marriage = full marital benefits including marital deduction, unlimited gifts, portability. Same planning as opposite-sex couples.

sections.title: Summary
items: QTIP trusts balance spouse and children interests in blended families
QDOTs allow marital deduction deferral for non-citizen spouses
Minor children need guardianship designations and structured trusts
Unmarried partners must explicitly plan—no automatic inheritance
Section 2503(c) and Crummey trusts qualify minor gifts for annual exclusion

sections.title: Blended Family Estate Planning: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold, remarried with children from a prior marriage, wants to ensure his current spouse, Wilma, is provided for after his death, but also wants to guarantee his children from his first marriage inherit the bulk of his assets eventually. Which of the following estate planning tools would BEST achieve this goal, while minimizing potential family conflict?
options: A simple will leaving all assets to Wilma, trusting she will eventually leave the remainder to Harold's children.
A Qualified Terminable Interest Property (QTIP) trust, naming Wilma as the income beneficiary and Harold's children as the remainder beneficiaries.
A durable power of attorney granting Wilma complete control over Harold's assets during his lifetime and after his death.
Leaving all assets jointly to Wilma and his children as joint tenants with right of survivorship.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A QTIP trust allows Harold to provide income to Wilma during her lifetime while ensuring the assets ultimately pass to his children. This addresses both his objectives and minimizes the risk of Wilma disinheriting his children. A simple will relies too heavily on Wilma's goodwill. A durable power of attorney is for lifetime management, not post-death distribution. Joint tenancy would give Wilma full ownership upon Harold's death, potentially disinheriting his children.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which option gives Harold the MOST control over the ultimate disposition of his assets.

reveal.question: What is the key limitation on the marital deduction when the surviving spouse is NOT a U.S. citizen?
reveal.answer: Generally, the marital deduction is NOT available for transfers to a non-citizen spouse UNLESS a Qualified Domestic Trust (QDOT) is used. The QDOT allows the marital deduction while ensuring that estate taxes are paid upon distributions from the trust or the spouse's death.

sections.title: Guardianship vs. Trust for Minor Children

rows.label: Purpose
values: Care and custody of a minor child.
Management of assets for a minor child.

rows.label: Management
values: Guardian manages the child's well-being and personal care.
Trustee manages the assets according to the trust document.

rows.label: Court Oversight
values: Generally requires ongoing court supervision.
May have less court supervision, depending on the trust terms.

rows.label: Asset Control
values: Guardian has limited control over assets unless appointed conservator as well.
Trustee has broad control over assets within the trust.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is a significant estate planning challenge faced by unmarried domestic partners compared to married couples?
options: Difficulty in establishing a durable power of attorney.
Automatic inheritance rights in the absence of a will.
Unlimited marital deduction for federal estate tax purposes.
Lack of default inheritance rights and limited access to certain benefits like Social Security spousal benefits.
Inability to create a valid will
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Unmarried domestic partners do not have the same default inheritance rights as married couples. They must actively plan to ensure their partner inherits their assets. They also do not qualify for the unlimited marital deduction or Social Security spousal benefits. Durable powers of attorney are available regardless of marital status, and anyone can create a will.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the legal rights and benefits automatically granted to married couples but NOT to unmarried partners.
